content.text: Who can read the logs, and can you prove who did?
Log access controls and audit logging

answer both questions: the first with role-based permissions and tenant isolation over the log platform, the second with an immutable record of every query and access event. Together they turn an open observability backend into a governed system that satisfies least-privilege and accountability requirements.
What it is

Log access control is the authorization model that governs which principals can read which logs, enforced at both the query layer (the dashboards and APIs engineers use) and the data plane (the object storage and databases underneath). Audit logging is the complementary control that records every access — who queried what, when, from where, and under what authorization — in a tamper-resistant store separate from the logs themselves.

The two are inseparable. Access control without audit logging enforces policy but cannot prove it was followed; audit logging without access control records violations but does not prevent them. A complete program combines role-based access, tenant or namespace isolation, and least-privilege defaults with an immutable, independently-stored audit trail of access events.
Why it matters

By default, most observability platforms grant every engineer read access to all logs, because that is the path of least resistance for debugging. But logs frequently contain sensitive operational and personal data, and open access violates the least-privilege principle that frameworks like SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR require. Auditors increasingly ask not just who is permitted to access logs, but who actually did — a question only an audit trail can answer. Without these controls, a single compromised engineer account or malicious insider can exfiltrate the entire log history unnoticed, and the organization cannot even scope the breach after the fact.
Benefits
Least-privilege access limits the blast radius of a compromised account to that account's authorized scope.
Tenant isolation prevents one team from reading another team's logs on a shared platform.
An immutable audit trail provides defensible evidence of access for auditors and investigations.
Access reviews become tractable because roles and their scopes are explicit and enumerable.
Anomalous-access detection is possible because every query is a recorded, analysable event.
Separation of query-layer and data-plane enforcement closes the direct-access bypass.
Trade-offs and limitations
Fine-grained access control slows debugging when an engineer lacks access to a log they suddenly need.
Enforcing controls at both the query layer and the data plane doubles the configuration surface.
The audit trail itself contains sensitive data (who looked at what) and needs its own protection.
Tenant isolation on a shared platform adds operational complexity and can fragment cross-cutting queries.
Role sprawl accumulates over time; without reviews, stale grants quietly re-open access.
How it works

Access is modelled as roles mapped to scopes. A role (engineer, support, security-auditor) is granted read access to a set of log scopes defined by labels — service, environment, sensitivity tier. The query layer enforces the mapping by injecting a mandatory label filter into every query so a principal physically cannot retrieve logs outside their scope. The data plane enforces the same mapping with storage-level permissions (bucket policies, database row/namespace security) so that direct access through the SDK or console is equally constrained. Every access — a dashboard query, an API call, a direct object read — emits an audit event to a separate, append-only store.

The audit store is designed to be more tamper-resistant than the logs it protects. It lives in a separate trust domain (a different account or project), is write-only for the systems that emit to it, and uses object-lock or equivalent immutability so that even an administrator cannot rewrite history. A meta-audit records attempts to modify the audit store itself.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Access requests enforced at query and data planes, with every access written to an immutable audit store

content.text: A principal's role and scope claims are evaluated at the query layer, which injects a mandatory filter, and again at the data plane through storage permissions. Both layers emit an audit event for every access to a separate immutable store. Because enforcement is dual-layered, a principal cannot bypass the query layer by hitting storage directly, and every path is recorded.
When to use it
Any shared observability platform serving multiple teams or trust levels.
Deployments under SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, or GDPR that require least-privilege and access logging.
When logs contain personal, financial, or otherwise sensitive data beyond pure machine metrics.
Overkill for a single-team, single-tenant system with only non-sensitive infrastructure logs — but audit logging is still cheap insurance.
How to implement
Define a role taxonomy and map each role to log scopes expressed as label selectors.
Enforce scope at the query layer by injecting a mandatory label filter into every query.
Mirror the same scope at the data plane with storage IAM, namespace, or row-level policies.
Emit an audit event for every access from both layers, capturing principal, scope, query, and time.
Store audit events in a separate trust domain with append-only, immutable configuration.
Add a meta-audit and alerting for any attempt to modify the audit store.
Run periodic access reviews and anomaly detection over the audit trail; revoke stale grants.
Examples

# Immutable audit event to a separate trust domain
import boto3, json, hashlib
from datetime import datetime, timezone

audit_s3 = boto3.client("s3")   # credentials scoped write-only to audit account

def audit_event(principal, scope, query):
    event = {
        "ts": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "principal": principal["id"],
        "role": principal["role"],
        "scope": scope,
        "query": query,
        "source_ip": principal.get("ip"),
    }
    body = json.dumps(event, sort_keys=True).encode()
    key = f"access/{event['ts']}/{hashlib.sha256(body).hexdigest()[:16]}.json"
    # Object Lock (WORM) makes the record immutable
    audit_s3.put_object(Bucket="log-access-audit", Key=key, Body=body,
                        ObjectLockMode="COMPLIANCE",
                        ObjectLockRetainUntilDate=retain_until())
Real-world example

A SaaS company ran a shared Loki/Grafana stack where all 300 engineers could query every tenant's logs. A SOC 2 audit flagged this as a least-privilege failure. They introduced three roles mapped to label scopes, put a proxy in front of Loki that injected the scope filter and wrote an audit event per query, and closed direct S3 access to the chunk store except for a single service role. Engineers now query only their own services' non-restricted logs; a small security team holds full scope.

When a customer later raised a data-access concern, the company produced the audit trail showing every query that had touched that customer's tenant labels over the prior year — principal, timestamp, and query text — and demonstrated that no unauthorized access had occurred. The audit store lived in a separate AWS account with Object Lock, so even the platform administrators could not have altered the record.
Common mistakes
Enforcing access only at the dashboard while leaving the underlying storage world-readable to engineers.
Logging access only at one layer, missing accesses that go directly to the data plane.
Keeping the audit trail in the same account and store as the logs, so an attacker can erase both.
Granting broad temporary access during an incident and never revoking it afterward.
Never running access reviews, so role grants accumulate until the model is meaningless.
Best practices
Enforce scope at both the query layer and the data plane; a single-layer control is bypassable.
Store the audit trail in a separate trust domain with immutable, append-only configuration.
Default new principals to the narrowest scope and widen only on demonstrated need.
Run scheduled access reviews and revoke grants that are no longer justified.
Analyse the audit trail for anomalies — bulk queries, off-hours access, unusual scopes.
Performance and scalability

Query-layer filter injection adds negligible latency — it is a string operation before the query runs — and the mandatory filter often narrows the scan, improving performance. The audit write is the main added cost; make it asynchronous and buffered so it never blocks the query response, shipping events to the immutable store via a queue. Audit volume equals query volume, which is far lower than log volume, so the audit store stays small relative to the logs. Access reviews scale with the number of roles, not principals, if roles are well-designed; keep the role count small and scope-based rather than minting a role per person.

Security and reliability

The audit trail is the last line of accountability, so its integrity outweighs its availability: it must be impossible to alter, even if that means it is briefly unavailable. Use a separate account, write-only credentials, and object-lock immutability, and enable a meta-audit that records any attempt to touch the audit configuration. Test immutability regularly by attempting a deletion and confirming it fails and is logged. For reliability, ensure the audit write path fails closed for high-sensitivity scopes — if an access to restricted logs cannot be audited, deny the access rather than allow an unrecorded read.
Detecting anomalous access

An audit trail is most valuable when it is analysed, not merely retained. Because every access is a structured event, simple heuristics catch the access patterns that precede data exfiltration: a principal querying far more scopes than usual, bulk queries returning unusually large result sets, access outside normal working hours, or a sudden widening of a support agent's session scope. These signals feed a low-volume review queue rather than a page, since they are indicators, not certainties.

attrs.alt: Audit events compared against per-principal baselines to surface anomalous access for review

content.text: These heuristics are deliberately simple and explainable; their purpose is to surface a small, reviewable set of unusual accesses, not to make automated access decisions, which would risk blocking legitimate incident response.
Mental model

Think of the log platform like a bank vault with a visitor log. Access control is the set of keys — each role's key opens only certain deposit boxes, and the same restriction applies whether you enter through the front desk (the query layer) or the loading dock (the data plane). The audit trail is the tamper-proof visitor log recording every entry, kept in a separate building so that someone who breaks into the vault cannot also erase the record of having done so. The bank reviews the log for suspicious visits, not just to react to thefts but to notice the reconnaissance that precedes them.
Tenant isolation on a shared platform

Most organizations run one observability platform shared across many teams, which makes tenant isolation the practical core of access control. Isolation ensures a query issued in one team's context can only ever return that team's data, even if the querier crafts a selector that tries to reach wider. The strongest implementations enforce isolation with a per-tenant identifier that the platform attaches to every stored record and requires on every read, so cross-tenant access is not a matter of policy but of physical data partitioning.

Isolation and role-based scope compose: the tenant boundary is the outer wall, and roles carve scopes within a tenant. A security-auditor role may span tenants for investigation, but every such cross-tenant read is an audited, exceptional event rather than a routine one.
Derive the tenant identifier from the authenticated identity, never from a request parameter.
Inject the tenant label at the platform layer so a crafted selector cannot escape it.
Compose tenant isolation (outer wall) with role scopes (inner partitions).
Treat any cross-tenant read as an exceptional, always-audited event.
Partition storage by tenant so isolation is physical, not merely a query convention.
Test isolation with an adversarial query that deliberately tries to reach another tenant's data.
Key takeaways
Combine role-based access control with an immutable audit trail; neither is sufficient alone.
Enforce scope at both the query layer and the data plane to close the direct-access bypass.
Store the audit trail in a separate trust domain with WORM immutability and a meta-audit.
Default to the narrowest scope and revoke incident-time access grants promptly.
Analyse the audit trail for anomalous access patterns, feeding a human review queue.
Make the audit write fail closed for restricted scopes: no audit, no access.
